region_of_origin: China (Cantonese / Southern Chinese tradition); served widely in Singapore
food_subgroup: Leafy green vegetables with seafood; stir-fried vegetable dish

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a pea shoot food product class (FOODON_03301489) but no specific class for stir-fried pea shoots with shrimp. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_description: Peas, pods and shoots — used as closest parent for dou miao (snow pea shoots) stir-fried dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for stir-fried dou miao with shrimp. A00LC (peas, pods and shoots) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept exists for stir-fried dou miao with shrimp. SNOMED CT food hierarchy does not extend to this dish level.

main_ingredients: dou miao (snow pea shoots / pea sprouts)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of stir-fried dou miao with shrimps is approximately 250 g, representing a shared vegetable dish for two to three diners at a Chinese restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Stir-fried pea shoots with shrimp — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central: Snow pea sprouts (FDC ID 169957) and Shrimp, cooked (FDC ID 175180), with cooking oil adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for stir-fried dou miao with shrimp exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data: snow pea sprouts (FDC ID 169957) at approximately 70% by weight and cooked shrimp (FDC ID 175180) at approximately 20% by weight, with approximately 10% contribution from cooking oil (vegetable oil, FDC ID 172336) and seasoning. Values are blended proportionally and represent a reasonable estimate for a typical restaurant preparation. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of green leafy vegetables and legume shoots — analogous estimate based on pea shoots / snow peas GI data; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried dou miao with shrimp. GI is estimated at 22 (low) by analogy with snow peas and pea shoots, which are low-GI legume vegetables with GI values in the range of 15–35 per the University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International Tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The dish is predominantly leafy green vegetable with shrimp protein; carbohydrate content is low, supporting a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (22 × 4.5 × 2.5) / 100 ≈ 2.5 g, confirming very low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each 250 g restaurant serving provides approximately 775 mg sodium, which represents about one-third of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced soy sauce or consume a smaller portion to manage sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and low carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a glycemic index of approximately 22 and only 4.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g, this dish has a very low glycemic load (approximately 2.5 per serving) and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as part of a balanced meal, though sodium from seasoning should still be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high vegetable content, lean shrimp protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish provides predominantly unsaturated fats, lean shrimp protein, and heart-protective phytonutrients from pea shoots including folate and vitamin C; however, sodium from soy sauce seasoning should be moderated in patients with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Dou Miao with Shrimps (虾仁豆苗) is a classic Chinese vegetable dish featuring dou miao — the tender young shoots and leaves of the snow pea or field pea plant — rapidly stir-fried in a wok over high heat with fresh shrimps, garlic, vegetable oil, and light seasoning such as soy sauce and salt. The dish is a signature offering at Din Tai Fung and other Cantonese-influenced Chinese restaurants in Singapore and across Southeast Asia. Dou miao has a delicate, slightly sweet flavour with a tender yet slightly crisp texture when properly wok-fried; the shrimps add a sweet, briny contrast and a source of lean protein. The high-heat wok technique (wok hei) imparts a characteristic smoky aroma. Nutritionally, the dish is low in carbohydrates (approximately 4.5 g per 100 g), moderate in protein (approximately 7.2 g per 100 g) from the shrimp component, and relatively low in saturated fat (approximately 0.6 g per 100 g). It provides dietary fibre, vitamin C, folate, iron, and vitamin A from the pea shoots. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 22 (low), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate to high due to soy sauce seasoning, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is halal-friendly and dairy-free. It is typically served as a shared vegetable side dish in a Chinese restaurant meal context.

part of a multi-dish Chinese family-style dinner
lunch or dinner at a Cantonese or Taiwanese restaurant
healthy vegetable option in a Chinese set meal

confidence_reason: No exact entry for stir-fried dou miao with shrimp was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional database. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for snow pea sprouts and cooked shrimp with cooking oil adjustment. GI is estimated by analogy with snow peas and pea shoots from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component foods are well-characterised in approved sources and the dish composition is straightforward.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central component data (snow pea sprouts FDC ID 169957, cooked shrimp FDC ID 175180, vegetable oil FDC ID 172336) blended at approximately 70:20:10 weight ratio. Actual restaurant preparation at Din Tai Fung may differ in oil quantity and seasoning. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central entries for the component foods and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. 3. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero based on the plant and seafood ingredient profile with no hydrogenated fats used. 4. GI is estimated at 22 (low) by analogy with snow peas and pea shoots per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (Diabetes Care, 2008). No direct GI measurement for this dish was found. 5. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Grains and staples' to 'Vegetables', which accurately reflects the primary ingredient (dou miao / pea shoots). 6. Sodium estimate of 310 mg per 100 g reflects typical restaurant-style seasoning with light soy sauce; home-prepared versions may be lower. 7. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (22 × 4.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 2.475, rounded to 2.5.
